questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate word(s) from the options given below to complete the following sentence.

We lost confidence in him because he never __________ the grandiose promises he had made.

A. delivered  B. delivered on  C. forgot  D. reneged on
questions.to_find: The most appropriate word(s) to complete the sentence about not fulfilling grand promises

understand.plain: The sentence says people lost confidence because the person never fulfilled huge promises. The blank must capture the idea of failing to carry through on promises. 'Delivered on' means to fulfil or make good on a commitment, which fits perfectly. 'Reneged on' means to go back on a promise, but that would imply betrayal rather than simply not following through.

keywords.explain: impressively large, ambitious, or elaborate in scale; often suggests something is overly ambitious
keywords.example: His grandiose plan to build a skyscraper never got past the blueprint stage.

keywords.explain: to fulfil or make good on a promise, commitment, or expectation
keywords.example: She promised a great performance and delivered on every expectation.

keywords.explain: to go back on or break a promise or agreement, often implying bad faith
keywords.example: He reneged on the deal at the last minute, leaving everyone disappointed.

understand.visual_alt: A sentence-completion question testing vocabulary related to promise-fulfilment.

given.aim: Find the phrase that means the person never fulfilled the large promises he made

terms.meaning: people expected fulfilment; their trust eroded because he failed to meet expectations
terms.example: We lost faith because promises were not kept.
terms.connects: points to the idea of not following through, not just forgetting or betraying

terms.meaning: made good on, fulfilled, honoured a commitment
terms.example: The team delivered on its promise to finish before the deadline.
terms.connects: the phrasal verb 'deliver on' means to fulfil a promise — negating it gives 'never delivered on'

terms.meaning: went back on, broke, repudiated a promise
terms.example: The politician reneged on his election pledge.
terms.connects: a plausible distractor but implies deliberate reversal; the sentence emphasises unfulfilled promises more broadly

given.plan: Look for a phrasal verb that means 'fulfilled'. Option B 'delivered on' = made good on promises; option D 'reneged on' = broke promises. The sentence says he 'never' completed the promise, so 'delivered on' (never delivered on) captures unmet expectation best.

steps.title: Understand what the sentence needs
steps.apply: The subject 'never _____ the grandiose promises' requires a verb phrase meaning 'fulfilled'. The context of lost confidence points to broken expectations, not mere forgetting.
steps.note: Option C 'forgot' is too weak — forgetting is not what destroys confidence in the context of grandiose promises.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A. 'delivered' alone does not idiomatically pair with 'promises' — you deliver on promises. B. 'delivered on' is the correct idiom: 'never delivered on the promises' = never fulfilled them. C. 'forgot' implies absence of memory, not failure to act. D. 'reneged on' means actively breaking a promise — possible but stronger than the neutral failure implied here.
steps.note: 'Delivered on' is the standard English idiom for fulfilling a promise and fits both grammatically and semantically.

solution.result: B. delivered on — 'never delivered on the grandiose promises' correctly expresses that he never fulfilled what he promised.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary question; no mathematical formulas apply.

questions.question:
Choose the word or phrase that best completes the sentence below.

__________ in the frozen wastes of Arctic takes special equipment.

A. To survive  B. Surviving  C. Survival  D. That survival
questions.to_find: The grammatically and semantically correct word or phrase to begin the sentence about surviving in the Arctic

understand.plain: The sentence requires a subject for the verb 'takes'. A noun or gerund can serve as a subject. 'Survival' is a plain noun and makes the sentence 'Survival in the frozen wastes of Arctic takes special equipment', which is grammatically perfect. Both 'To survive' (infinitive) and 'Surviving' (gerund) can technically serve as subjects, but 'Survival' is the most natural noun subject for a factual statement.

keywords.explain: a verb form ending in -ing that functions as a noun (e.g., 'Surviving takes courage')
the to + verb form used as the subject of a sentence (e.g., 'To err is human')
keywords.example: To err is human; to forgive, divine.

keywords.explain: a noun phrase acting as the subject or object of a sentence
keywords.example: Survival of the fittest is a well-known principle.

understand.visual_alt: A sentence-completion question testing knowledge of appropriate grammatical subjects.

given.aim: Identify the correct word or phrase to be the subject of the verb 'takes'

terms.meaning: the blank is at the start, so it must be the subject of 'takes'
terms.example: '_______ in the Arctic takes special equipment' — subject + prepositional phrase + verb
terms.connects: needs a noun, gerund, or infinitive phrase

terms.meaning: the state of continuing to live or exist, especially in difficult conditions
terms.example: Survival in extreme conditions depends on preparation.
terms.connects: as a countable/uncountable noun, 'survival in the Arctic' is a well-formed noun phrase serving as subject

terms.meaning: introducing a nominal clause 'that survival takes...' — but 'that survival' alone is not a complete clause and reads as a demonstrative determiner
terms.example: That survival — incorrect as a standalone nominal clause start without a verb following 'that'
terms.connects: option D is grammatically awkward; 'that' before a noun here makes the phrase incomplete

given.plan: All of A, B, C can act as subjects, but the question asks for the 'best' word or phrase. 'Survival' is the cleanest, most idiomatic noun subject for a general factual statement. 'To survive' (A) and 'Surviving' (B) both make slightly less natural subjects than the noun 'Survival' in this formal register.

steps.title: Identify grammatical roles
steps.apply: The blank is the sentence subject; 'takes special equipment' is the predicate. Options A (infinitive), B (gerund), and C (noun) can all be subjects. Option D ('That survival') would require a full clause like 'That survival is possible takes...' — it does not work cleanly.
steps.note: Eliminating D leaves A, B, C as candidates.

steps.title: Select the most natural subject
steps.apply: In formal, factual English, a plain noun ('Survival') is the most natural and direct subject. 'Surviving in the frozen wastes...' (B) is correct but slightly informal; 'To survive in the frozen wastes...' (A) has an infinitive subject which is stylistically less preferred. 'Survival in the frozen wastes of Arctic takes special equipment' reads smoothly and precisely.

solution.result: C. Survival — 'Survival in the frozen wastes of Arctic takes special equipment' is the most natural and grammatically clean sentence.

questions.formula_note: This is a grammar and vocabulary question; no mathematical formulas apply.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate word from the options given below to complete the following sentence.

The __________ of evidence was on the side of the plaintiff since all but one witness testified that his story was correct.

A. paucity  B. propensity  C. preponderance  D. accuracy
questions.to_find: The most appropriate word meaning 'the greater quantity or weight' of evidence favouring the plaintiff

understand.plain: The sentence tells us that almost all witnesses supported the plaintiff, meaning the bulk or majority of evidence favoured him. 'Preponderance' means the quality of being greater in number, quantity, or importance. 'Preponderance of evidence' is also a standard legal phrase meaning the greater weight of evidence, making option C the perfect fit.

keywords.explain: the quality of being greater in number, quantity, power, or importance; in law, the larger share of evidence
keywords.example: The preponderance of scientific studies supports the vaccine's safety.

keywords.explain: a scarcity or lack of something; smallness of number
keywords.example: There was a paucity of evidence against the defendant.

keywords.explain: a natural inclination or tendency to behave in a certain way
keywords.example: He had a propensity for exaggeration.

understand.visual_alt: A sentence-completion question testing legal and general vocabulary about the weight of evidence.

given.aim: Find the noun that describes the large amount or greater weight of evidence in favour of one party

terms.meaning: nearly all witnesses agreed, so the evidence was overwhelmingly on one side
terms.example: All but one jury member voted guilty — an overwhelming consensus.
terms.connects: points to a word meaning 'majority' or 'greater weight', not 'scarcity' or 'tendency'

terms.meaning: a legal standard meaning the greater weight or amount of credible evidence in favour of one party
terms.example: Under the preponderance of evidence standard, the plaintiff need only show it is more likely than not.
terms.connects: matches the context perfectly: almost all witnesses = preponderance favours plaintiff

terms.example: A paucity of witnesses harmed the prosecution's case.
terms.connects: opposite of what is described; the sentence says evidence was strong, not scarce

given.plan: Eliminate paucity (means scarcity — opposite of what is described). Eliminate propensity (means tendency — does not collocate with 'of evidence' in this sense). Eliminate accuracy (means correctness — not about quantity or weight). Select preponderance (the greater weight/majority of evidence).

steps.title: Interpret the context
steps.apply: The sentence says 'all but one witness' testified for the plaintiff — meaning almost all evidence pointed one way. The blank needs a noun meaning 'great quantity or majority portion' of evidence.
steps.note: The phrase '_______ of evidence was on the side of' requires a noun that collocates with 'evidence' and means 'majority' or 'greater weight'.

steps.title: Match word meanings to context
steps.apply: A. paucity = shortage — contradicts the idea that many witnesses agreed. B. propensity = tendency — does not fit as a descriptor of evidence volume. C. preponderance = greater weight/majority — 'preponderance of evidence' is a set legal phrase matching the context. D. accuracy = correctness — describes quality, not quantity.
steps.note: 'Preponderance of evidence' is the standard legal phrase for the standard of proof in civil cases.

solution.result: C. preponderance — 'The preponderance of evidence was on the side of the plaintiff' correctly expresses that the majority (almost all witnesses) supported the plaintiff.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary question; no mathematical formulas apply.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate alternative from the options given below to complete the following sentence.

The administrators went on to implement yet another unreasonable measure, arguing that the measures were already __________ and one more would hardly make a difference.

A. reflective  B. utopian  C. luxuriant  D. unpopular
questions.to_find: The word that best describes the existing measures as already objectionable, so one more would not change much

understand.plain: The administrators justify adding another unreasonable measure by saying the existing measures are already in a certain state, so one more will not matter. If the measures were already 'unpopular', then adding another unpopular one does not significantly worsen the situation — this is the logic of their argument. The word must describe the measures in a way that makes 'one more would hardly matter' logically coherent.

keywords.explain: not liked or supported by most people; generally disliked
keywords.example: The tax hike was deeply unpopular among voters.

keywords.explain: idealistically perfect but unrealistic or impractical
keywords.example: His utopian vision of a crime-free city was impossible to realise.

keywords.explain: showing careful consideration or thought; also, capable of reflecting light
keywords.example: She gave a reflective pause before answering.

understand.visual_alt: A sentence-completion question about administrators justifying additional unpopular measures.

given.aim: Find the adjective that makes the administrators' argument logically consistent: if measures are already X, one more X measure hardly matters

terms.meaning: the argument is: existing measures are already [negative quality]; adding one more [negative quality] measure is marginal
terms.example: If rules are already complicated, one more rule is not much worse.
terms.connects: the blank must be a negative or undesirable quality so that 'one more would hardly matter'

terms.meaning: not liked or supported; facing opposition
terms.example: Administrators argued that since measures were already unpopular, one more would not worsen public opinion significantly.
terms.connects: fits the logic: already unpopular + one more = still unpopular; the marginal addition does not matter

terms.example: A utopian plan for zero poverty sounds fine but cannot be implemented.
terms.connects: being already utopian does not explain why one more measure would not matter; logic is weak

given.plan: The administrators need a word that justifies their 'one more won't matter' argument. 'Unpopular' works: if measures are already disliked, one more disliked measure is a marginal addition to existing opposition. Other choices (reflective, utopian, luxuriant) do not fit this 'already at a negative extreme' logic as well.

steps.title: Understand the argument structure
steps.apply: The administrators say: 'measures are already _____ so one more won't make a difference.' For this reasoning to hold, the blank must represent a negative characteristic at saturation — adding one more of the same type won't change much.
steps.note: 'Unpopular' creates the 'already saturated with opposition' logic the argument needs.

steps.title: Evaluate the distractors
steps.apply: A. reflective — describes thoughtfulness; has no 'one more won't matter' implication. B. utopian — impractically idealistic; does not explain why adding more measures is fine. C. luxuriant — means lush and rich growth (usually of vegetation); clearly off-context. D. unpopular — the measures face opposition; one more unpopular measure adds marginally to existing unpopularity.

solution.result: D. unpopular — 'measures were already unpopular and one more would hardly make a difference' captures the administrators' (flawed) justification for adding yet another disliked policy.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary and reasoning question; no mathematical formulas apply.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate alternative from the options given below to complete the following sentence.

To those of us who had always thought him timid, his __________ came as a surprise.

A. intrepidity  B. inevitability  C. inability  D. inertness
questions.to_find: The word that contrasts with 'timid' to explain what was surprising about the person

understand.plain: The sentence tells us that people who thought this person was timid (fearful, easily frightened) were surprised by a certain quality he showed. The blank must be an antonym or strong contrast to 'timid'. 'Intrepidity' means fearlessness and courage — the exact opposite of timidity — which would logically come as a surprise to those who believed him cowardly.

keywords.explain: showing a lack of courage or confidence; easily frightened
keywords.example: The timid student rarely spoke up in class.

keywords.explain: fearlessness; the quality of being bold and daring, not deterred by danger
keywords.example: Her intrepidity in the face of danger earned her a medal for bravery.

keywords.explain: lack of activity, movement, or reaction; sluggishness
keywords.example: The chemical's inertness made it safe to handle.

understand.visual_alt: A sentence-completion question requiring a word that contrasts with 'timid'.

given.aim: Find the noun that is the opposite of timidity and would surprise observers who believed the person was fearful

terms.meaning: lacking courage; the established expectation about the person
terms.example: He was always seen as timid and unlikely to take risks.
terms.connects: the blank must be its antonym to create the 'surprise' mentioned in the sentence

terms.meaning: remarkable fearlessness; bold courage in the face of danger
terms.example: The soldier's intrepidity during the battle was celebrated by everyone.
terms.connects: directly contrasts with timid — fearlessness is the opposite of timidity

terms.meaning: the quality of being certain to happen; unavoidability
terms.example: The inevitability of change is something we must accept.
terms.connects: has no logical contrast with 'timid' and does not explain why it would come as a surprise

given.plan: Identify the word that is an antonym of 'timid'. Intrepidity = fearlessness is the direct opposite of timidity. Check other options: inevitability (fate), inability (lack of ability), inertness (lack of activity) — none of these contrast with timidity. Only A creates the logical surprise.

steps.title: Identify the contrast required
steps.apply: People believed him timid. Something about him 'came as a surprise'. The surprise must be a quality opposite to timidity. The blank is that contrasting quality.
steps.note: The surprise works because it violates expectations: a timid person displaying fearlessness (intrepidity) is unexpected.

steps.title: Match options to the antonym of timid
steps.apply: A. intrepidity = fearlessness, boldness — directly opposes timidity. B. inevitability = certainty of occurrence — no contrast with timid. C. inability = lack of ability — could contrast with competence, not timidity. D. inertness = sluggishness, inactivity — related to laziness not fear.
steps.note: Intrepidity is the only word among the options that is a direct antonym of timidity.

solution.result: A. intrepidity — 'his intrepidity came as a surprise' correctly describes how his fearlessness was unexpected to those who had always seen him as timid.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary question; no mathematical formulas apply.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate pair of words from the options given below to complete the following sentence.

The high level of __________ of the questions in the test was __________ by an increase in the period of time allotted for answering them.

A. difficulty, compensated  B. exactitude, magnified  C. exertion, mitigated  D. attitude, mitigated
questions.to_find: The pair of words that correctly describes the high complexity of questions being balanced out by extra time allotted

understand.plain: The sentence says the test questions had a high level of some quality, and this was addressed or balanced by giving more time to answer. The first blank needs a noun describing a challenging quality of questions. The second blank needs a verb meaning 'balanced out' or 'offset'. 'Difficulty, compensated' fits perfectly: difficulty is increased, and extra time compensates for it.

keywords.explain: the quality of being hard to do, understand, or deal with
keywords.example: The high level of difficulty in the entrance exam required months of preparation.

keywords.explain: made up for, offset, or balanced out something negative
keywords.example: The extra pay compensated for the long hours worked.

keywords.explain: made less severe, serious, or painful
keywords.example: The judge's leniency mitigated the severity of the sentence.

understand.visual_alt: A two-blank sentence-completion question about test difficulty and the response of giving extra time.

given.aim: Find the pair: (noun for the challenging quality of questions) and (verb for what extra time did to offset this quality)

terms.meaning: needs a noun that describes a measurable property of test questions at a 'high level'
terms.example: High level of difficulty / high level of complexity
terms.connects: difficulty collocates naturally with 'high level of' and 'test questions'

terms.meaning: needs a past-tense verb meaning the extra time balanced or offset the high difficulty
terms.example: Extra time compensated for the difficulty.
terms.connects: 'compensated by' = was offset by; if difficulty is high, more time compensates

terms.meaning: exactitude = extreme precision; magnified = made larger
terms.example: The exactitude required was magnified by strict marking.
terms.connects: magnified means increased — it would mean extra time made the precision requirements even greater, which is logically opposite to the intended meaning

given.plan: For blank 1: test questions at a 'high level' of ___; candidates are 'difficulty', 'exactitude', 'exertion', 'attitude'. Only 'difficulty' naturally collocates with test questions at a high level. For blank 2: the measure 'was _____ by extra time'; candidates are 'compensated', 'magnified', 'mitigated', 'mitigated'. 'Compensated' and 'mitigated' both mean offset/reduced; 'magnified' means increased. Since blank 1 is 'difficulty', 'compensated by extra time' = extra time offsets the difficulty. Choose A.

steps.title: Fill blank 1: quality of test questions
steps.apply: The sentence says 'high level of _____ of the questions'. Options for blank 1: difficulty (hardness), exactitude (extreme precision), exertion (physical/mental effort), attitude (mindset). 'High level of difficulty of the questions' is the standard collocation for hard test questions. 'Exertion' and 'attitude' do not collocate well with 'questions'. 'Exactitude' is possible but unusual in this context.
steps.note: Difficulty is by far the most natural word for measuring how hard test questions are.

steps.title: Fill blank 2: effect of increased time allowance
steps.apply: The blank comes in the passive: 'was _____ by an increase in time'. The blank must mean 'balanced out' or 'offset'. Options: compensated (offset/balanced), magnified (increased — wrong direction), mitigated (lessened — also possible). Pairing with difficulty: 'high level of difficulty was compensated by extra time' is a natural and correct expression. 'Mitigated' works logically too, but Option C pairs it with 'exertion' (which is weaker for blank 1).
steps.note: 'Compensated for by' is the standard idiom: extra time compensates for high difficulty.

solution.result: A. difficulty, compensated — 'The high level of difficulty of the questions was compensated by an increase in the period of time allotted for answering them' is the most precise and idiomatic completion.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary and sentence-completion question; no mathematical formulas apply.
